fields.Identifying_Data_Chief_Complaint: 13-year-old brought by teacher after finding self-harm cuts and suicide note in backpack during routine search.
fields.Subjective: Patient is a 13-year-old presenting after teacher found fresh cuts on paper towel and typed suicide note during backpack search for missing assignment. Note detailed plan to hang self in garage. Patient initially denied but then confirmed cuts (on thighs, hidden) and note, stating wrote it 'to get feelings out' but not sure about intent. Reports overwhelming feelings of worthlessness, believing family would be better off without the burden of their mental health struggles. Has been cutting thighs for 3 months. Currently on fluoxetine 20mg for anxiety started 2 months ago. Sees therapist monthly. Academic performance good. Has close friend group. Supportive mother who was unaware of cutting or suicidal thoughts. No prior suicide attempts. (Psychiatrist Dr. Steven Walsh, MD)

Mental_Status_Examination.Thought_Content: Active SI with written plan but uncertain intent; NSSI ongoing; feelings of being burden

fields.Diagnostic_Impression: Major depressive disorder; generalized anxiety disorder; suicidal ideation with plan; non-suicidal self-injury.
fields.Psychosocial_Context: Hidden cutting for 3 months; suicide note with plan; current medication (2 months); monthly therapy; good academics; close friends; supportive mother unaware.
Assessment_and_Plan: Moderate-to-high risk: documented suicide plan, ongoing NSSI, uncertain intent.
Some protective factors: friends, mother support, engaged in treatment.
Mother to secure garage access and hanging means.
Current medication dose likely insufficient; only 2 months trial.
Increase therapy frequency from monthly to weekly minimum.
Address feelings of being burden; cognitive distortions.
Mother education on monitoring and recognizing warning signs.
Consider brief psychiatric admission versus intensive outpatient.
Discharge contingent on means restriction, therapy intensification, possible medication increase.
fields.Author: Dr. Steven Walsh, MD (Psychiatrist)
